title: Consent capture evidence freshness — stale-consent alerting
exclusions: Does NOT evaluate whether the consent text itself meets Art.7 transparency — that is a legal review, handled outside the platform.
evidence: Daily 'consent_freshness' search writes per-subject consent age and scope to compliance_summary; panel shows distribution of consent ages by lawful basis.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Art.7 conditions for consent — the UC evidences when consent was captured and surfaces subjects whose consent is older than the policy threshold.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that GDPR Art.7 (Conditions for consent) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.37.1: Consent capture evidence freshness — stale-consent alerting.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.37.1' running on catalogue-defined data sources (see UC detailedImplementation), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Art.6(1)(a) lawful basis of consent requires demonstrable consent. Missing or stale evidence means no lawful basis.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that GDPR Art.6 (Lawful basis) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.37.1: Consent capture evidence freshness — stale-consent alerting.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.37.1' running on catalogue-defined data sources (see UC detailedImplementation), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Derived from GDPR@2016/679 Art.7 (parent assurance='partial'). UK GDPR preserves parent clause numbering; inherited assurance degraded one step.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that UK GDPR Art.7 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.37.1: Consent capture evidence freshness — stale-consent alerting.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.37.1' running on catalogue-defined data sources (see UC detailedImplementation), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Derived from GDPR@2016/679 Art.6 (parent assurance='partial'). UK GDPR preserves parent clause numbering; inherited assurance degraded one step.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that UK GDPR Art.6 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.37.1: Consent capture evidence freshness — stale-consent alerting.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.37.1' running on catalogue-defined data sources (see UC detailedImplementation), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: When a subject's active consent grant is older than the configured max_consent_age_days (default 365) and the processing activity is still observed in application logs, the UC fires.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Processing activities with lawful basis ≠ consent (e.g. Art.6(1)(c) legal obligation) do NOT fire.

spl:
index=cmp sourcetype=onetrust:consent earliest=-400d
| stats max(_time) as last_consent by subject_token purpose
| eval consent_age_days=round((now()-last_consent)/86400,0)
| where consent_age_days>365
| join subject_token [search index=app_activity earliest=-30d | stats count by subject_token | where count>0]
| table subject_token purpose last_consent consent_age_days
description: Flags subjects whose consent is older than the program threshold yet whose data is still being processed. Stale consent is a lawful-basis failure — Art.6/Art.7 require consent to remain current and demonstrable.
value: Prevents the 'set-and-forget' consent anti-pattern that surfaces in nearly every GDPR supervisory-authority enforcement action.
implementation: (1) Ingest CMP events with purpose taxonomy; (2) set max_consent_age_days via macro; (3) run daily; (4) pipe to SOAR to drive a re-consent workflow.
visualization: Histogram of consent age by purpose; table of stale subjects; single-value for '% of processing with fresh consent'.
knownFalsePositives: Long-lived contractual relationships where consent is not the lawful basis. Use a lawful_basis.csv join to filter them out.
grandmaExplanation: We help you prevent the 'set-and-forget' consent anti-pattern that surfaces in nearly every GDPR supervisory-authority enforcement action — so you can show exactly what is being watched and when something went wrong.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Enterprise / Splunk Cloud Platform.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: See subcategory preamble..
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Ingest CMP events with purpose taxonomy; (2) set max_consent_age_days via macro; (3) run daily; (4) pipe to SOAR to drive a re-consent workflow.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=cmp sourcetype=onetrust:consent earliest=-400d
| stats max(_time) as last_consent by subject_token purpose
| eval consent_age_days=round((now()-last_consent)/86400,0)
| where consent_age_days>365
| join subject_token [search index=app_activity earliest=-30d | stats count by subject_token | where count>0]
| table subject_token purpose last_consent consent_age_days
```

Understanding this SPL

**Consent capture evidence freshness — stale-consent alerting** — Prevents the 'set-and-forget' consent anti-pattern that surfaces in nearly every GDPR supervisory-authority enforcement action.

Documented **Data sources**: See subcategory preamble. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Enterprise / Splunk Cloud Platform. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: cmp; **sourcetype**: onetrust:consent. If that sourcetype is not mentioned in Data sources, double-check parsing or update the documentation to match the feed you actually ingest.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=cmp, sourcetype=onetrust:consent, time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by subject_token purpose** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions (useful for per-host, per-user, or per-entity comparisons for this use case).
• `eval` defines or adjusts **consent_age_days** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• Filters the current rows with `where consent_age_days>365` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• Joins to a subsearch with `join` — set `max=` to match cardinality and avoid silent truncation.
• Pipeline stage (see **Consent capture evidence freshness — stale-consent alerting**): table subject_token purpose last_consent consent_age_days


Step 3 — Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Histogram of consent age by purpose; table of stale subjects; single-value for '% of processing with fresh consent'.
